During glycolysis, locally stored muscle glycogen, and possibly some blood-borne glucose, supply the substrate for energy generation. Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm, where no oxygen is required, so the process is called anaerobic. It has been referred to as 'anaerobic lactic', since lactic acid is formed as the end product. Sufficient lactic acid formation can lower the pH of the cell (i.e. make it more acid) to the extent that further energy production may be reduced.

The major substrate for anaerobic glycolysis (see equation below) is glycogen stored within the muscle, so prior hard exercise without adequate repletion of glycogen will limit further high-intensity short-term work.

## Definition

Anaerobic glycolysis is the cytoplasmic catabolism of glucose/glycogen to lactic acid (lactate) without the requirement for oxygen, generating ATP for short-term high-intensity work.

## Core Principles

- The process occurs in the cytoplasm of the cell, distinct from oxidative metabolism which takes place in the mitochondria.
- The reaction is anaerobic (oxygen-independent) and produces lactic acid as the end product, hence the term 'anaerobic lactic'.
- Accumulation of lactic acid lowers intracellular pH (greater acidity), which can impair further energy production. The pH scale provides the framework for describing this acidification, with lower pH values denoting more acidic conditions (acidic vs alkaline).
- ATP generated via glycolysis serves as the immediate energy currency of the cell for muscle contraction during high-intensity short-term exercise. ATP's role within the cell is to act as the universal energy carrier, coupling exergonic catabolic steps to endergonic cellular work.
- Energy transfer in glycolysis involves phosphorylation/dephosphorylation reactions and oxidation/reduction reactions, characteristic exergonic chemical reactions that release usable energy (in contrast to endergonic reactions which require energy input). A chemical reaction is defined as a process in which substrates are transformed into products via bond rearrangement, and can be classified as endergonic (energy-absorbing) or exergonic (energy-releasing).
- Glycolysis as a chemical pathway is built from common reaction types — synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and oxidation/reduction — operating on substrates composed of the main chemical elements of the human body and held together by ionic and covalent bonds.

## Sub-concepts

- Substrates: muscle glycogen (primary) and blood-borne glucose (secondary).
- End product: lactic acid / lactate.
- Site: cytoplasm.
- Cellular context: the plasma membrane regulates entry of blood-borne glucose; the cytoplasm houses the glycolytic enzymes; the nucleus contains the genetic instructions for glycolytic enzyme expression; the mitochondria are not involved in the anaerobic phase. Basic cell structure thus partitions glycolysis (cytoplasm) from oxidative metabolism (mitochondria), with the plasma membrane as the regulated boundary and the nucleus as the genetic control centre.
- Acid–base chemistry: lactic acid behaves as an acid, dissociating to lower pH; bases and salts together with buffering systems counter this shift, framed by acid/base/salt definitions and the pH scale. Acids release H⁺ in solution, bases accept H⁺ or release OH⁻, and salts arise from acid–base neutralisation; the pH scale quantifies H⁺ concentration with acidic values below 7 and alkaline values above 7.
- Solution chemistry: substrate and product concentrations are expressed using mole, molar and molarity; water acts as the cellular solvent (owing to its polar chemical property) in which glycolytic reactions proceed. A mole denotes a fixed number of particles (Avogadro's number), molar refers to one mole per litre, and molarity expresses solute concentration in moles per litre.
- Atomic/molecular basis: atoms, ions, molecules and compounds participate in glycolysis, with basic atomic structure underpinning bond formation and breakage during the pathway. Atoms consist of a nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons; ions are charged atoms; molecules are two or more atoms bonded together; compounds are molecules of two or more different elements.
- Bonding: ionic bonds form via electron transfer producing charged species, whereas covalent bonds form via electron sharing; both bond types feature in glycolytic intermediates.
- Energy units: the energy released by exergonic glycolytic steps and captured in ATP is quantified using standard units of energy. Energy is defined as the capacity to do work, with standard SI units (joules) used to quantify metabolic energy yield.
- Water functions: beyond acting as the cellular solvent, water participates in transport, temperature regulation and as a reactant/product in hydrolysis and condensation steps relevant to glycolytic substrate handling; its solvent capability stems from its polar chemical property.

## Practical Applications

- Prior hard exercise without adequate repletion of glycogen will limit further high-intensity short-term work, making glycogen restoration a practical priority between bouts.
- Monitoring/managing intracellular pH (e.g. via training adaptations or buffering strategies) addresses the limitation imposed by lactic acid accumulation.
